experiences.intentions:
Player 1: I am Merlin and know 2 and 4 are evil, so team [0,1] contains me and excludes known evil, making it a strong opening team. In team voting, good should generally approve clean teams early, especially quest 1 with only 2 members. Approving reveals little beyond normal support for a safe team and helps good secure an early pass.
Player 0: I have no concrete role information yet from quest outcomes or voting patterns. As a Good Servant on round 1, approving an initial 2-person team that includes me is standard and helps generate information without risking much; rejecting gives little benefit this early. My approve vote reveals a normal pro-information stance and does not significantly hurt Good.
Player 3: As a Servant, I have no hidden information beyond behavior so far. On round 1 with no prior data, a 2-person team of leader plus one other is standard and includes me in neither slot, but that alone is not suspicious. Early game approvals are generally good to gather quest information, and rejecting without evidence gives little benefit. My vote reveals I am willing to test a normal opening team, which helps Good collect data from the quest result.
Player 2: I know only that Player 4 is my evil teammate; players 0, 1, and 3 are good-aligned unknowns from my perspective. The current phase is team voting on Quest 1, and the proposed team [0,1] contains no evil players, so if approved it is likely to succeed and build trust between two good players. As evil, early successful all-good teams can still help hide us, but approving a clean team also gives good valuable information and momentum. Since it's round 1 and a rejection is low-cost, rejecting avoids endorsing a guaranteed good team and keeps options open without revealing much, because early rejections are common.
Player 4: I know player 2 is evil with me; players 0, 1, and 3 are nominally unknown to me. The proposed team [0,1] contains no evil players, so if approved it will likely succeed and give good side a clean early pass. As evil in the team voting phase, optimal play is usually to reject all-good teams, especially on quest 1, to try to get an evil onto a future team and create ambiguity. My reject slightly signals opposition to this clean team, but on round 1 that's acceptable and consistent with broad caution.

experiences.intentions:
Player 3: Quest 1 succeeded with team [0,1], so 0 and 1 are somewhat more trustworthy though not confirmed. As a good Servant on team voting, the optimal play is to approve a reasonable team that includes proven/somewhat-cleared players and myself. Team [0,1,3] keeps the successful pair and adds me, which is strong for Good and reveals little harmful information.
Player 1: I am Merlin and know 2 and 4 are evil. Proposed team [0,1,3] excludes both known evil players, which is exactly what Good wants on quest 2. Approving also helps establish a clean team and does not reveal extra information beyond a reasonable good vote.
Player 0: Quest 1 succeeded with team [0,1], so both 0 and 1 have some positive signal, though one could still be evil who chose to pass. Player 3 is untested. As a good Servant in team voting on quest 2, approving a team that keeps the two successful prior members and adds one new player is generally strong and information-efficient. My vote reveals trust in the strongest available evidence and helps advance the game without needlessly burning rejections.
Player 4: I know player 2 is my evil teammate, and I am evil. The proposed team [0,1,3] excludes both evil players, so if approved it is very likely to succeed and give Good a second point. As evil in team voting, I should reject strong all-good teams, especially after [0,1] already succeeded and now 1 adds 3, which may build a trusted good bloc. My reject slightly signals resistance to this clean team, but preventing or contesting a likely success is worth it.
Player 2: I know player 4 is my evil teammate, so this proposed team [0,1,3] contains no evil players and would certainly succeed, giving Good a second point. As evil in team voting, I want to prevent clean good teams from going through when possible. Rejecting also aligns with preserving ambiguity; approving a fully good team only helps Good and reveals little useful for Evil. Since it's early in quest 2 round 1, there is no pressure from the 5-rejection rule.

experiences.intentions:
Player 1: I am Merlin and know 2 and 4 are Evil. The proposed team is exactly [2,4], so approving would guarantee giving Evil control of the quest and lose valuable information tempo. Since Good is up 2-0, the optimal play is to reject any team containing known Evil, especially both of them together. My reject vote is consistent with a good player avoiding a bad team and does not expose more than necessary.
Player 2: I know Player 4 is my evil teammate; players 0,1,3 are likely good based on two clean successes from teams [0,1] and [0,1,3]. As evil on team vote, the optimal play is to approve a team containing both evil players so we can secure a fail on quest 3. Approving is also consistent with me proposing this team and does not reveal extra information beyond my support for my own proposal.
Player 4: I know player 2 is evil with me, and both prior successful quests were on teams [0,1] and [0,1,3], so 0/1/3 look relatively good from public info. We are in team voting on quest 3, and as evil down 0-2, getting an all-evil 2-person team approved is excellent because we can force a fail and stay alive. Voting approve is optimal for evil and publicly consistent with wanting to support a team that includes myself and the proposer. My vote reveals little since evil and good could both plausibly approve here.
Player 3: Two successful quests with teams [0,1] and [0,1,3] give some positive evidence that 0, 1, and especially I (3) are good, though evil can hide early. The proposed team [2,4] excludes all proven-success participants and pairs the two least-cleared players, which is high risk on quest 3. As a good Servant, optimal play is to reject risky teams and prefer teams anchored by prior successes. My reject signals that this composition is suspicious and preserves pressure to form a safer team next round.
Player 0: Quests 1 and 2 both succeeded with teams [0,1] and [0,1,3], so players 0, 1, and likely 3 look relatively good; at least neither included an observed fail. The new team [2,4] excludes all previously successful players and pairs the two least-cleared players, which is high risk for Good. As a Servant, optimal play is to reject teams that discard established successful members without reason, especially on quest 3 where Evil needs to start creating failures. Rejecting also signals preference for continuity with trusted players and does not meaningfully help Evil beyond public skepticism they already see.

experiences.intentions:
Player 1: I am Merlin and know 2 and 4 are evil. The proposed team [0,3] excludes known evil players and both prior successful quests used 0,1 and 0,1,3, so 0 and 3 are strongly good candidates. In team voting, approving a clean team helps Good secure the third success while revealing little harmful information beyond supporting a sensible team.
Player 3: Two successful quests included players 0,1 on Q1 and 0,1,3 on Q2, so 0 and 1 look fairly good and I know I am good. Team [0,3] is a subset of a successful quest team and should be very safe for a 2-person mission. As a good servant in team voting, optimal play is to approve strong evidence-based teams; rejecting would add noise and risk. Approving also reinforces a consistency standard around successful teams and reveals pro-good preference rather than helping evil.
Player 4: I know player 2 is evil with me, and this proposed team [0,3] contains no evil players, so it is very likely to succeed and move Good to 3-0. With evil already down 0-2, my optimal play is to reject any clean-looking team and try to force a different composition later, ideally including 2 or 4. My vote reveals some resistance to a double-good core, but as evil I need to block successful teams more than preserve perfect cover here.
Player 0: Two successful quests with teams [0,1] and [0,1,3] suggest 0,1,3 are all likely good, though one evil could be hiding by passing. Player 2 proposed [2,4], which was rejected by a majority, giving slight suspicion to 2/4. Current phase is team voting on [0,3]; as a good servant with 2-0 lead, approving a team composed of players from successful teams is optimal and low risk. My vote reveals trust in a historically successful pair, which helps good consolidate wins more than it helps evil.
Player 2: Players 0 and 1 were on two successful quests, so they look relatively good; 3 was also on the second success, so [0,3] is a strong-looking all-good team from the table's perspective. As evil Minion, I want quest 3 to fail to stop good from reaching 3 successes, but this proposed team excludes both evil players 2 and 4, so if approved it likely succeeds and puts evil in a very bad spot. My vote itself reveals little because rejecting a clean-looking team is somewhat suspicious, but preventing/trying to prevent this team is worth it. Optimal evil play is to reject and hope enough others do too, allowing a future team with an evil player included.
